coredata.dc:title: Portable arginine sensing on polyvinyl alcohol membrane based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dou
Shao, Tong
Li, Peng
Wang, Xiaoshuang
Zeng, Suyuan
Hong, Min
Li, Rui
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were highly promised nanomaterials for application in food analysis, owing to their exceptional optical properties, superior photostability, and negligible biological toxicity. N-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized via a straightforward hydrothermal method utilizing folic acid as only one precursor material. NCDs exhibited deep blue fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp with a quantum yield (QY) of 14.54 %. In the presence of arginine (Arg), there was a noticeable redshift of 10 nm in the maximum emission wavelength of NCDs, accompanied by fluorescence transition from dark blue to bright blue. The QY of NCDs was 16.71 % in the presence of Arg. In other words, NCDs showed aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) in the presence of Arg. The fluorescence enhancement exhibited a strong linear correlation with Arg concentration within the range of 0–300 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.31 μM. Furthermore, to facilitate Arg detection, a portable Arg sensor was developed using a polyvinyl alcohol membrane-assisted design. The sensor demonstrated satisfactory performance in detecting Arg in real samples. These findings highlighted the versatile capabilities of NCDs with AIEE properties, paving the way for their potential applications in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arginine
Aggregation-induced emission enhancement
Carbon dots
Polyvinyl alcohol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Arg in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of NCDs (inset: photos of NCDs under natural and UV lamp), (B) influence of excitation wavelength on fluorescence spectra of NCDs, (C) determination of QY and (D) fluorescence spectra of NCDs and NCDs + Arg.

HRTEM images of (A) NCDs and (B) NCDs + Arg (inset: Size distribution of NCDs + Arg), (C) XRD pattern, (D) FT-IR spectrum and (E) Full survey XPS spectrum of NCDs, and HR-XPS patterns of (F) C1s, (G) N1s, and (H) O1s.
(A) Effect of amino acid on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs, (B) the anti-interference for Arg detection, and (C) photos of NCDs and NCDs + Arg with amino acid in UV light.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs with different concentrations of Arg, (B) linear relationship between F/F0 and Arg concentration, and (C) photos of NCDs + Arg in UV light.
Size distribution of (A) NCDs and NCDs + Arg (B) with various concentrations of Arg, (C) Zeta potential of NCDs, Arg and NCDs + Arg, (D) Fluorescence decay of NCDs and NCDs + Arg.
Synthesis of NCDs and their application in Arg detection.

head.ce:title: Portable arginine sensing on polyvinyl alcohol membrane based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Luqiao Group Co. Itd, Jinan, 14677, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Luqiao Group Co. Itd, Jinan 14677, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were highly promised nanomaterials for application in food analysis, owing to their exceptional optical properties, superior photostability, and negligible biological toxicity. N-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized via a straightforward hydrothermal method utilizing folic acid as only one precursor material. NCDs exhibited deep blue fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp with a quantum yield (QY) of 14.54 %. In the presence of arginine (Arg), there was a noticeable redshift of 10 nm in the maximum emission wavelength of NCDs, accompanied by fluorescence transition from dark blue to bright blue. The QY of NCDs was 16.71 % in the presence of Arg. In other words, NCDs showed aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) in the presence of Arg. The fluorescence enhancement exhibited a strong linear correlation with Arg concentration within the range of 0–300 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.31 μM. Furthermore, to facilitate Arg detection, a portable Arg sensor was developed using a polyvinyl alcohol membrane-assisted design. The sensor demonstrated satisfactory performance in detecting Arg in real samples. These findings highlighted the versatile capabilities of NCDs with AIEE properties, paving the way for their potential applications in food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Arginine (Arg), as an essential amino acid, is essential for maintaining cellular function, promoting wound healing, strengthening the immune system, and regulating blood pressure [1–8]. Arg is not only an essential component of protein synthesis, but also a precursor of a variety of biologically active substances, such as nitric oxide, polyamines and polyamides [9–12]. In addition, it is also involved in DNA repair and methylation modification, and has a profound impact on the stability of genetic information and expression regulation [13]. Arg attracts lots of attention in clinical diagnosis and scientific research owing to its significant importance on human physiological system. Thus, it is useful to construct an effective method to detect Arg. There are various fluorescent methods for the determination of Arg with different probes. They are carbazole [14], calixpyridinium [15], 1,1′-binaphthol [16], polystyrene sulfonate and pyrene methyl ammonium chloride [17], hemicyanine derivative [18], rhodamine B modified UiO-66-NH2 MOFs [19], thiazole [20], as well as carbon dots (CDs) [21–25] as fluorescent probes and NaYF4-gold nanoparticle [26] as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) systems. However, none of these methods concerned aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE).

AIEE is a phenomenon where certain luminophores exhibit enhanced fluorescence when aggregated, in contrast to conventional quenching seen in most materials. AIEE is closely linked to the rigidity of the molecular structure, where the restricted molecular rotation and vibration reduce non-radiative decay pathways, leading to stronger fluorescence [27]. CDs, a type of fluorescent nanomaterial with size below 10 nm, have garnered significant attention due to their tunable optical properties, biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness [28]. In recent years, the AIEE effect in CDs has become an area of active research, particularly for applications in bioimaging, sensors, and optoelectronics [29]. Unlike many fluorescent materials that suffer from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), certain CDs show improved emission when they aggregate or form clusters, which is attributed to the restriction of intramolecular motion and the increased radiative decay [30]. This unique behavior is often tuned by controlling the surface functional groups, the synthesis process, or the introduction of specific solvent and environment [31].

Various substances and environmental factors can promote the aggregation of CDs, leading to AIEE. Commonly, solvent, metal ion, polymer, and organic molecule are key triggers for inducing aggregation and fluorescence enhancement. For instance, addition of other solvent like ethanol or isopropanol to CDs aqueous solution can reduce their solubility, promoting aggregation and subsequently enhancing fluorescence due to the restriction of molecular motion. Similarly, the introduction of metal ion like Fe3+, Cu2+, or Ag+ can coordinate with functional groups on the surface of CDs, causing them to aggregate and increased emission [32,33]. Polymer, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) or polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), can also interact with CDs, leading to self-assembly into clusters and enhanced fluorescence through AIEE [34]. Furthermore, organic molecule like cyclodextrin or surfactant can encapsulate CDs and induce aggregation by modifying their surface properties [35]. These materials work by either directly interacting with the surface groups of CDs or by altering their dispersion state, which leads to the emission enhancement typically seen in AIEE process. There is no report about the AIEE of CDs caused by Arg.

In this study, a comprehensive study on the AIEE phenomenon exhibited by nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) was proposed and the exploitation for the sensitive and selective detection of Arg (Scheme 1 ). NCDs were synthesized using folic acid as the carbon and nitrogen source through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. NCDs exhibited deep blue fluorescence in ultraviolet (UV) light. In the presence of Arg, the fluorescence of NCDs shifted to bright blue, accompanied by a remarkable increase in fluorescence quantum yield (QY) from 14.54 % to 16.71 %. The fluorescence intensity enhancement of NCDs showed a linear response to the concentration of Arg in the range from 0 to 300 μM, with the limit of detection (LOD) at 0.31 μM. To further facilitate Arg detection, a portable Arg sensor was developed using a PVA membrane-assisted design. The sensor demonstrated satisfactory performance in detecting Arg in real samples, suggesting that the proposed method holds significant promise for real-time Arg detection in food products.

NCDs was prepared in a DZF-6050 vacuum drying oven (Bongxun, China) using a Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor. Fluorescence spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) of NCDs were obtained using F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and LUH4150 UV–Vis (Hitachi, Japan). The morphology and size of NCDs were studied by F200X electron microscope (HRTEM, Therom Scientific Ltd., USA). The crystal structure of NCDs was confirmed by D/Max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). The chemical valence states of NCDs elements were analyzed by K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). Raman spectra were obtained by Raman spectrometer (Spectroscopy & Imaging (S&I), BRD). Thermogravimetric analysis data were acquired via a Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA, NETZSCH Gerätebau GmbH., BRD). The results of dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential were obtained by a Nanoparticle Size Analyzer (NSA, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The Coolpix 4500 Digital camera (Nikon, Japan) was used to take digital photos. High performance liquid chromatography graphs were recorded on a UltiMate 3000 DGLC (HPLC, Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA).

The chemicals used in the experiment included Arg, proline (Pro), leucine (Leu), histidine (His), lysine (Lys), glutamic acid (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Trp), alanine (Ala), serine (Ser), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and quinine sulfate (QS). All the reagents were purchased from McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd. in Shanghai, China, and used directly without further purification. The solutions used in the experiment were all prepared with deionized (DI) water.

NCDs were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method in this work (Scheme 1). Simply, 100 mg FA was added to 20 mL DI water and dissolved completely with ultrasound for 20 min. Then, the solution of FA was transferred to a 25 mL high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature, the dispersion of NCDs was collected. NCDs dispersion was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to remove the impurities of large particles and filtered with a 0.22 μm needle filter to remove the carbonized precipitate. Finally, NCDs dispersion was dialyzed in 1000 Da dialysis bags for 48 h to remove the unreacted raw materials and by-products. NCDs solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The QY of NCDs was measured by referring to the previous literature [36]. In a word, the QY of NCDs was measured using quinine sulfate (0.1 M, Φst = 54 %, ηst = 1.33) as fluorescence reference. First, the absorbance at 360 nm and the integral fluorescence intensity at 463 nm were measured, and the QY of NCDs was calculated using eq. S(1) with detailed information in S1.1 (in Supporting information).

NCDs solution was diluted 100 times with DI water for subsequent experiments. Before the detection of Arg, the stability of NCDs and NCDs + Arg was investigated at diverse pH values (2-12) and different time (0–72 h). The pH values of NCDs and NCDs + Arg were adjusted by the diluted stock solution of HCl (5 %) and NaOH (5 %). The detection solution of NCDs with or without Arg was kept at room temperature, and the fluorescence intensity was measured for no longer than 6 h.

For the determination of Arg, a series of Arg calibration solutions were first prepared with different concentrations. Second, 1 μL Arg standard solution (0–450 μM) was added to 250 μL NCDs diluent and the mixture was shaken well. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of all samples were recorded in the wavelength range of 400–600 nm (λ ex 360 nm).

First, PVA (100 mg/mL) was added to NCDs solution in a beaker, which was placed in water bath for heating at 90 °C with continuous stirring. After complete dissolution, urea (65 mg/mL) was added to the mixture as a crosslinker. Then, the beaker was removed from water bath while it was hot. Finally, the mixture was spread on a transparent glass plate and cooled naturally at room temperature until the water completely evaporated and a composite film was formed [36].

To verify the practical application of the present method, Arg was detected in human complex Arg capsules and animal amino acid liquid feed. First, the sample was diluted with DI water and used directly without further pretreatment. Arg in the sample of human body tonic was determined directly by the proposed method while the recovery was studied after the sample of animal feed was spiked with Arg. To confirm the accuracy of the approach, HPLC was employed as a control method [37].

The optical properties of NCDs were investigated through the measurement of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The absorption spectrum of NCDs reveals one distinct peak at 330 nm (Fig. 1 A), which corresponds to n–π∗ transition of imine units [38]. NCDs solution appears pale yellow and transparent in natural light, while it exhibits dark blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1A). Notably, there is no significant change in the maximum emission wavelength as the excitation wavelength increases from 350 nm to 370 nm (Fig. 1B). The maximum emission at 463 nm can be observed under excitation of 360 nm, which are optimal wavelength parameters, respectively. As indicated in Fig. 1C, the QY of NCDs is 14.54 %, while that of NCDs + Arg increases to 16.71 %. As shown in Fig. 1D, there is an obvious red-shift in the emission peak of NCDs from 463 nm to 473 nm after the addition of Arg. The fluorescence intensity is significantly enhanced, and the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 473 nm is about 2.2. As illustrated in Fig. S2A (in Supporting Information), the recognition of Arg by NCDs is impeded in an acidic environment, while the optimum condition of pH is about 7. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs and NCDs + Arg remains about 90 % within 36 h and 80 % within 72 h, respectively (Fig. S2B, in Supporting Information).

The morphological and surface characteristics of NCDs were studied by HRTEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, Raman and TGA techniques. First, it can be observed that the morphology of NCDs is near-spherical and monodisperse with the average particle size at 2.78 nm as shown in Fig. S1A (in Supporting Information). Furthermore, the HRTEM images of NCDs indicate that their lattice is 0.14 nm (Fig. 2 A), revealing the graphite-like structure of CDs [39]. In the presence of Arg, there is obvious aggregation of NCDs with the average particle size increased by 9.05 nm in Fig. S1B (in Supporting Information). It is also verified by HRTEM that the average size of NCDs + Arg is about 11.83 nm without distinct crystal structure (Fig. 2B). The XRD pattern shows that there is a wide peak at 21.5°, which can be ascribed to mainly amorphous structure of NCDs [40] (Fig. 2C).

Fig. 2D displays the functional groups of NCDs analyzed by FT-IR. The absorption peak at approximately 3215.13 cm−1 corresponds to OH/NH vibrations and good hydrophilicity of NCDs is owing to the functional groups of OH and NH2 [41]. The absorption peaks at 1676.21 cm−1 and 1402.44 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of CO and C–N, respectively [41,42]. The peak at 1115 cm−1 may be ascribed to the C–O–C asymmetric stretching vibration [25]. Additionally, the absorption peak around 717.50 cm−1 is possibly attributed to the bending vibration of C–H [43]. It can be inferred that there are rich and diverse functional groups on the surface of NCDs.

The elemental composition of NCDs was characterized using XPS. The full XPS spectrum of NCDs reveals three distinct peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2E). HR-XPS of C1s (Fig. 2F) exhibits peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV and 288.6 eV, which can be ascribed to the CO, CC/C–C and C–O/C–N groups [44]. The HR-XPS of N1s (Fig. 2G) shows three peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.6 eV and 400.5 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and N–H, respectively [45]. Furthermore, HR-XPS of O1s (Fig. 2H) owns two peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.3 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of CO and C–O–H, respectively [46]. The XPS analysis of NCDs reveals CC, CO, C–N, C–C, COOH and OH groups on the surface of NCDs. This finding is consistent with that of FT-IR analysis.

Raman spectrum of NCDs is shown in Fig. S3A (in Supporting Information), in which there exhibits a peak at 1587 cm−1, which is identified as "graphene band". It indicates the existence of sp2 hybridization pattern [47]. The thermal stability of NCDs was evaluated by TGA, as shown in Fig. S3B (in Supporting Information). The results indicate that the quality of NCDs remains nearly constant when it approaches 800 °C, the weight undergoes a reduction of approximately 56.99 % throughout the heating process. It may due to the surface of NCDs encompassing a considerable number of OH and COOH groups. These functional groups give rise to the excellent water solubility of NCDs. Thus, the product of NCDs is difficult to dry, and the moisture is significantly reduced throughout the heating process [48].

The specificity of NCDs for Arg detection was discussed. First, a series of standard solutions of amino acid (37.5 mM) were prepared, including Pro, Leu, His, Lys, Glu, Phe, Trp, Ala, Ser, and Arg. Second, 250 μL NCDs solution was individually added to the above amino acid solutions (2 μL 300 μM). As shown in Fig. 3 A, it can be observed that only Arg can cause the fluorescence of NCDs to change from dark blue to bright blue along with significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity. Subsequently, a 1:1 ratio (300 μM) of Arg and other amino acid solutions were simultaneously added to the NCDs solution to assess their fluorescence intensity.

As depicted in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs is enhanced in various amino acid solutions with Arg, demonstrating that NCDs exhibit excellent anti-interference property for Arg detection. The photos also provide intuitive proof (Fig. 3C), which are captured under a UV lamp (365 nm). They reveal that bright blue fluorescence can only be seen in NCDs + Arg solution, which indicates there is minimal interference from other amino acids for Arg determination. In short, NCDs exhibit remarkable selectivity and anti-interference capabilities to detect Arg.

As the concentration of Arg increases from 0 to 300 μM, the fluorescence spectrum of NCDs exhibits a redshift in their emission peak from 463 nm to 473 nm, accompanied by a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4 A). As shown in Fig. 4B, there is a linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F/F0) and Arg concentration within the range of 0–300 μM (R2 = 0.9998). It can be described by the linear equation (F/F0) = 0.33[Arg]+0.69. According to IUPAC guidelines, the limit of detection (LOD) for Arg detection using 3SB/m can be calculated at 0.31 μM. In this formula, S B represents the standard deviation of the measurement (n = 5) and m denotes the average value of the sample. It demonstrates that NCDs exhibit high sensitivity in detecting Arg. More importantly, as the Arg concentration increases, the fluorescence of NCDs can be observed with naked eye from dark blue to bright blue under UV lamp (Fig. 4C). This provides favorable conditions for the development of portable Arg sensing method. Moreover, the results for Arg detection using fluorescence method are shown in Table S1 (in Supporting Information). By comparison, NCDs show a wider linear range and lower LOD than most other materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Arg in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: As an indispensable amino acid, Arg not only plays a crucial role in the human physiological system, but also serves as a vital component in biological protein synthesis [7,10]. Human complex Arg capsules and animal amino acid liquid feed are conducted to assess the suitability of the proposed method. Two approaches are used for the two real samples after pretreatment according to the experimental procedure [24]. The same samples are also studied by HPLC method, which is employed as the control method. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is between 0.31 % and 0.88 % and the recovery is in the range of 97.9 %–100.2 % as listed in Table S2 (in Supporting Information). Moreover, the results obtained by the present method are in excellent agreement with those of HPLC, demonstrating the reliability of this approach for detecting Arg in real samples.

As depicted in Fig. S4 (in Supporting Information), the NCDs-PVA film exhibits light-yellow in natural light and dark blue fluorescence in UV light. Upon feed of Arg solution with different concentrations (0–300 μM) on the NCDs-PVA film, it is evident that the fluorescence turns brighter and brighter with increase of Arg concentration, accompanied by a shift from dark blue to bright blue. The application of the NCDs-PVA film is also successful in real sample solutions containing Arg. It can be observed that NCDs-PVA film shows a significant fluorescence enhancement in real samples containing Arg (Fig. S4, in Supporting Information).

NCDs-PVA film, coated with Arg solution, can be dried at room temperature and subsequently treated with 1 % AcOH, saturated NH4Cl solution, saturated NaHCO3 solution, and saturated NaOH solution. The fluorescence of NCDs-PVA film is illustrated in Fig. S5 (in Supporting Information). The NCDs-PVA film covered with an acidic solution exhibits weak fluorescence. It is due to the formation of imine between NCDs and Arg, which is unstable under acidic condition and prone to hydrolysis, resulting in fluorescence quenching [46]. However, the reusability of the sensor is not affected. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs-PVA film can be recovered with an alkaline solution. To further explore the reusability of the sensor, NCDs-PVA film can be coated with Arg solution (300 μM) or animal feed additive solution (50 μM). After NCDs-PVA film with Arg is completely dry, it is treated with 1 % AcOH and in this case the film can be reused three times (Fig. S6, in Supporting Information). Therefore, through acidic treatment, a fluorescent "on-off-on" mode can be achieved for the NCDs-PVA film, thereby enabling its reusability.

The possible mechanism may be ascribed to AIEE for fluorescence enhancement of Arg on NCDs. First, the images of HRTEM indicate that the particle size of NCDs + Arg increase by approximately 9 nm (Fig. S1, in Supporting Information) [49]. DLS results also show that the size of NCDs is increased significantly in the presence of Arg (300 μM) (Fig. 5 A). Furthermore, NCDs are larger and larger with the increase of Arg concentration from 0.7 nm to 5.44 nm, 49.51 nm and 611.1 nm (Fig. 5B) [30]. It can be deduced that Arg can cause the aggregation of NCDs. Then, the potential of NCDs reveals a change from 1.39 to −1.25 mV in the presence of Arg (Fig. 5C), indicating an electrostatic interaction between NCDs and Arg [25]. In addition, the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs + Arg displays that the absorption peaks of CO, C–N and CC rise, which maybe the consequence of the interaction between Arg and NCDs [50]. The fluorescence lifetime of NCDs is significantly enhanced by about 2.11 ns from 8.29 ns to 10.40 ns in the presence of Arg (Fig. 5D) [27,32]. Based on these findings, the fluorescence enhancement of NCDs is probably due to the AIEE of NCDs in the presence of Arg.

In this study, NCDs were prepared using FA as only one precursor through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. NCDs exhibit dark blue fluorescence with a QY of 14.54 %. Based on the AIEE, a significant aggregation of NCDs takes place in the presence of Arg, leading to enhanced fluorescence. Based on this principle, the specific detection of Arg is achieved. Additionally, a portable sensor is developed based on NCDs modified PVA membrane to realize a rapid response to Arg, which can be reused three times. Compared with other methods, this approach shows a broader detection range and a lower LOD. Furthermore, it demonstrates a certain potential for the development of portability, providing a valuable reference for the detection of Arg.

Dou Yang: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tong Shao: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Peng Li: Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiaoshuang Wang: Validation, Investigation. Suyuan Zeng: Supervision, Conceptualization. Min Hong: Supervision, Conceptualization. Rui Li: Supervision, Conceptualization. Qiaoli Yue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by China Scholarship Council, China (202208370086), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2022MB052) and Graduate Education Quality Improvement Plan of Shandong Province, China (SDYJG21198), and Liaocheng University, China (318050022 and 318012116).
